title: The AI skills divide in Europe and Central Asia: Who benefits and who gets left behind
gist: A World Bank Education AI Readiness Assessment across Bulgaria, Romania, and Türkiye finds AI adoption in schools is outrunning teacher and student skill-building, so gains concentrate among students who already have strong digital/critical-thinking skills while others risk falling further behind, with teachers — not tool access — the pivotal bottleneck.

insights.claim: Turkish students use the state-backed EBA Student Assistant for personalized remediation, while a companion AI Teacher Assistant delivers integrated translation support to over one million school-age Syrian refugee children.
insights.speaker: Ciro Avitabile, Cristóbal Cobo, Rita Almeida, and Alberto Munoz-Najar
insights.so_what: A concrete case of state AI infrastructure being repurposed for refugee-inclusion at scale, a template for other countries with large displaced-population caseloads.
insights.quote: provides integrated translation support for over one million school-age Syrian refugee children.
insights.locator: Turkish students are actively using the state-backed EBA Student Assistant

insights.claim: Bulgaria built BgGPT, the first large-scale generative AI model trained entirely on the Bulgarian language, specifically to remove the English-language barrier for local learners, alongside the Ucha.se platform's over 27,000 gamified, curriculum-aligned video lessons and quizzes.
insights.speaker: Ciro Avitabile, Cristóbal Cobo, Rita Almeida, and Alberto Munoz-Najar
insights.so_what: An emerging-market example of sovereign, localized-LLM investment aimed at education access — a model other non-English-language markets may replicate.
insights.quote: the first large-scale generative AI model trained entirely on the Bulgarian language
insights.locator: In Bulgaria, students are using Ucha.se

insights.claim: Romania's SARO learning assistant, used by students preparing for the high-stakes Baccalaureate exam, has boosted homework completion rates by 28%.
insights.speaker: Ciro Avitabile, Cristóbal Cobo, Rita Almeida, and Alberto Munoz-Najar
insights.so_what: One of the few quantified engagement effects in the piece — a measurable proof point for AI-tutoring ROI in a national exam context.
insights.locator: Romanian students are using learning assistants like SARO

themes.theme: Skills, not access, is the binding constraint

insights.claim: The central thesis is that the future of AI in education will be shaped less by access to technology and more by whether education systems build the critical-thinking and self-directed-learning skills needed to use it well.
insights.speaker: Ciro Avitabile, Cristóbal Cobo, Rita Almeida, and Alberto Munoz-Najar
insights.so_what: Reframes education-AI investment theses away from device/platform access toward curriculum and teacher-capacity spend as the actual value driver.
insights.quote: The future of AI in education will be shaped less by access to technology and more by how education systems build the right skills to use it.
insights.locator: emerging evidence from the World Bank-led Education AI Readiness Assessment

insights.claim: Forthcoming research (Oakley et al.) finds students with strong foundational cognitive skills use AI more effectively, while those who treat it as a crutch risk falling further behind, meaning AI adoption widens rather than narrows the pre-existing skills gap.
insights.speaker: Ciro Avitabile, Cristóbal Cobo, Rita Almeida, and Alberto Munoz-Najar
insights.so_what: A caution against treating classroom AI rollout as inherently equalizing; edtech investment cases premised on AI 'democratizing' learning need a skills-building complement to actually work.
insights.quote: students with strong foundational cognitive skills use AI more effectively, while those who treat it as a crutch risk falling behind

themes.theme: Policy investment is outrunning teacher capacity

insights.claim: Bulgaria, Romania, and Türkiye have each adopted ambitious national AI strategies for education (Bulgaria's National AI Concept 2020-2030, Romania's National AI Strategy 2024-2027, Türkiye's AI in Education Policy Document and Action Plan 2025-2029), but teacher training in AI remains uneven and often theoretical rather than practical.
insights.speaker: Ciro Avitabile, Cristóbal Cobo, Rita Almeida, and Alberto Munoz-Najar
insights.so_what: Ambitious national strategy documents are a weak proxy for classroom readiness; the training-quality gap is the real execution risk for these programs.
insights.quote: Teacher training in AI remains uneven and often theoretical rather than practical
insights.locator: All three countries have made major investments in infrastructure

insights.claim: Teacher upskilling has scaled fast on paper — Türkiye trained over 157,000 educators via its Teacher Information Network (ÖBA) and Romania reskilled nearly 83,000 teachers, almost half its active teaching workforce, via a National Recovery and Resilience Plan-funded 'Digital Pedagogy' initiative — but the decisive bottleneck is whether teachers can use AI to build students' higher-order thinking, not merely operate the tools.
insights.speaker: Ciro Avitabile, Cristóbal Cobo, Rita Almeida, and Alberto Munoz-Najar
insights.so_what: Large reported training-headcount numbers can mask a much smaller pool of teachers who can actually deploy AI pedagogically — a reason to discount headline upskilling stats when assessing program impact.
insights.quote: The more decisive bottleneck is whether teachers can use these tools to develop students' higher-order cognitive skills
insights.locator: Yet this momentum is constrained by a key challenge

insights.claim: National examinations in Bulgaria, Romania, and Türkiye rarely assess the higher-order skills — critical thinking, information literacy, ethical reasoning — that AI use demands, which removes schools' incentive to integrate AI meaningfully even where tools and training exist.
insights.speaker: Ciro Avitabile, Cristóbal Cobo, Rita Almeida, and Alberto Munoz-Najar
insights.so_what: An assessment-design fix (exams that test higher-order skills) is a cheap lever policymakers could pull to align incentives with the strategy documents already in place.
insights.quote: national examinations in all three countries rarely assess the higher-order skills
insights.locator: Meanwhile, systems are moving faster on technology deployment

insights.claim: The authors' priority policy package is: embed AI literacy across school curricula, provide continuous hands-on teacher training (pre- and in-service), redesign assessments to explicitly measure critical thinking and student agency, and address inequities in access to both technology and skills.
insights.speaker: Ciro Avitabile, Cristóbal Cobo, Rita Almeida, and Alberto Munoz-Najar
insights.so_what: A concrete checklist for evaluating whether a given country's AI-in-education program is likely to deliver, versus one that is technology-deployment-only.
insights.locator: What this means for the future

insights.claim: The authors call for rigorous evidence (randomized controlled trials or equivalent) before committing to national AI rollouts, and for structured public-private partnerships — citing Türkiye's ETKİM hub, which brings together government, industry, and educators — so EdTech innovation serves pedagogical need rather than commercial interest.
insights.speaker: Ciro Avitabile, Cristóbal Cobo, Rita Almeida, and Alberto Munoz-Najar
insights.so_what: Flags ETKİM as a specific institutional model worth tracking for how governments structure EdTech-vendor relationships to avoid commercial capture.
insights.quote: no education system can navigate this transformation alone
insights.locator: Yet even these actions will only deliver results

insights.claim: Without institutional capacity to monitor what AI is actually doing in classrooms and correct course before inequalities harden, the authors warn AI in education becomes 'less a solution than a very expensive gamble.'
insights.speaker: Ciro Avitabile, Cristóbal Cobo, Rita Almeida, and Alberto Munoz-Najar
insights.so_what: A blunt downside framing that cuts against edtech-optimist narratives — worth weighing against vendor claims of guaranteed learning gains from AI deployment.
insights.quote: AI in education is less a solution than a very expensive gamble.
insights.locator: True readiness, then, is not a race to deploy more tools
